Definition: Language-specific libraries (Python/Boto3, Java, Node.js, .NET, Go) that provide APIs to interact with AWS services programmatically — handling authentication, retries, pagination, and serialization for developers.
A unified tool to manage AWS services from the command line — supporting all AWS API operations, scripting automation, output formatting (JSON, table, text), and profile-based credential management.

Virtual servers in the cloud with configurable CPU, memory, storage, and networking — supporting On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, and Savings Plans pricing, with Auto Scaling for dynamic capacity management.

A service available at [IP_ADDRESS] that provides instance information (instance ID, AMI, security credentials, user data) — IMDSv2 requires session tokens (PUT then GET) for enhanced security against SSRF attacks.

A script (bash or cloud-init) that runs automatically when an EC2 instance first launches — used for bootstrapping tasks like installing software, configuring services, and pulling application code from repositories.

A serverless compute service that runs code in response to events without provisioning servers — supporting multiple runtimes (Python, Node.js, Java, .NET, Go, Ruby), with automatic scaling, pay-per-invocation pricing, and up to 15-minute execution time.

The runtime container for Lambda functions — includes a /tmp directory (up to 10 GB), environment variables, layers, and a lifecycle of INIT (cold start), INVOKE (handler execution), and SHUTDOWN phases.

The latency incurred when Lambda creates a new execution environment — includes downloading code, starting the runtime, and running initialization code. Mitigated with Provisioned Concurrency, smaller packages, and keeping connections outside the handler.
ZIP archives containing libraries, custom runtimes, or dependencies shared across multiple Lambda functions — up to 5 layers per function, reducing deployment package size and enabling code reuse.

Versions are immutable snapshots of function code and configuration. Aliases are pointers to specific versions — enabling blue/green deployments by shifting traffic between versions (e.g., alias 'prod' pointing to v2 with 90/10 weighted routing).
Key-value pairs configured on a Lambda function that are available to your code at runtime — supporting encryption with KMS for sensitive values like database credentials and API keys.

A feature that routes the result of asynchronous Lambda invocations to a destination (SQS, SNS, Lambda, EventBridge) based on success or failure — replacing custom error-handling code with declarative routing.

Configurations that read from stream/queue sources (Kinesis, DynamoDB Streams, SQS) and invoke Lambda with batches of records — managing polling, batching, error handling, and checkpointing automatically.

The number of function instances serving requests simultaneously — default 1,000 per region (soft limit). Reserved concurrency guarantees capacity for a function; Provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes environments to eliminate cold starts.

Configuring Lambda to access resources in a VPC (RDS, ElastiCache, private APIs) — Lambda creates ENIs in specified subnets using Hyperplane, requiring proper security group and NAT Gateway configuration for internet access.

A fully managed service for creating, publishing, and managing REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs — handling authentication, throttling, caching, request/response transformation, and API versioning at any scale.

Term: API Gateway - REST API vs HTTP API
Definition: REST APIs offer full feature set (caching, request validation, WAF, resource policies, usage plans). HTTP APIs are simpler, cheaper, faster, with JWT authorizers and OIDC support — choose HTTP API unless you need REST-specific features.

Named references to deployment snapshots of an API (dev, staging, prod) — each stage has its own URL, stage variables (like environment variables), canary deployments, and independent throttling/logging settings.

Rate limiting to protect backend services — default 10,000 requests/second with 5,000 burst across all APIs per region. Configurable per-stage and per-method throttling, with usage plans and API keys for client-level limits.

An optional cache (0.5 GB to 237 GB) that stores backend responses for a configurable TTL (default 300 seconds) — reducing backend calls and latency for repeated requests, with cache invalidation via Cache-Control headers.

A simplified integration where API Gateway passes the entire HTTP request as a JSON event to Lambda and expects a specific response format (statusCode, headers, body) — the most common integration pattern for serverless APIs.
A fully managed serverless NoSQL key-value and document database — providing single-digit millisecond performance at any scale, with on-demand or provisioned capacity modes, and automatic scaling.

The unique identifier for each item — Partition Key only (simple) or Partition Key + Sort Key (composite). The partition key determines data distribution; the sort key enables range queries and ordering within a partition.

Provisioned throughput measurement — 1 RCU = one strongly consistent read (up to 4 KB) or two eventually consistent reads. 1 WCU = one write (up to 1 KB). On-Demand mode auto-scales without capacity planning.

GSI (Global Secondary Index): different partition/sort key, own throughput, eventually consistent. LSI (Local Secondary Index): same partition key, different sort key, shares table throughput, supports strongly consistent reads. LSIs must be created at table creation.

Query retrieves items by partition key (and optional sort key condition) — efficient and targeted. Scan reads every item in the table — expensive and slow. Always prefer Query; use parallel scan and ProjectionExpression to optimize Scans when unavoidable.

Write operations (PutItem, UpdateItem, DeleteItem) with condition expressions that only succeed if conditions are met — enabling optimistic locking, preventing overwrites, and implementing atomic counters without race conditions.

An ordered flow of item-level changes (insert, update, delete) from a DynamoDB table — triggering Lambda functions for event-driven processing, cross-region replication, and real-time analytics with 24-hour retention.
A fully managed in-memory cache for DynamoDB that delivers up to 10x read performance — microsecond latency for cached reads, API-compatible (drop-in replacement), with item cache and query cache.

A feature that automatically deletes expired items based on a timestamp attribute — reducing storage costs and table size without consuming write capacity, with deletions appearing in DynamoDB Streams.

ACID-compliant operations across multiple items and tables using TransactWriteItems and TransactGetItems — supporting all-or-nothing operations for financial transactions, order processing, and multi-table updates.

Object storage with virtually unlimited scalability — storing objects (up to 5 TB) in buckets with unique keys, supporting versioning, lifecycle policies, encryption, event notifications, and multiple storage classes.

Time-limited URLs that grant temporary access to private S3 objects — generated using SDK/CLI with configurable expiration, enabling secure file uploads/downloads without requiring AWS credentials from the client.

A feature for uploading large objects in parts (5 MB to 5 GB each) — recommended for objects over 100 MB, enabling parallel uploads, pause/resume, and improved throughput. Required for objects over 5 GB.

Automated triggers when objects are created, deleted, or restored — sending events to Lambda, SQS, SNS, or EventBridge for processing pipelines, thumbnail generation, and data transformation workflows.

A fully managed message queuing service — Standard queues offer unlimited throughput with best-effort ordering and at-least-once delivery. FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and strict ordering (300 TPS, 3,000 with batching).

The period after a message is received during which it is hidden from other consumers — default 30 seconds, max 12 hours. If processing fails before timeout expires, the message becomes visible again for retry.
A queue that receives messages that fail processing after a configured number of attempts (maxReceiveCount) — enabling isolation and analysis of problematic messages without blocking the main queue.

A technique where SQS waits up to 20 seconds for messages before returning an empty response — reducing empty responses and API costs compared to short polling (immediate return), configured via WaitTimeSeconds.

A fully managed pub/sub messaging service — publishers send messages to topics, and subscribers (Lambda, SQS, HTTP, email, SMS) receive copies. Supports message filtering, FIFO topics, and fan-out patterns.

Publishing a message to an SNS topic that delivers copies to multiple SQS queues — enabling parallel, independent processing of the same event by different consumers with guaranteed delivery and decoupling.

A real-time data streaming service — producers send records with partition keys to shards (1 MB/s in, 2 MB/s out per shard), consumers process data in order per shard, with 24-hour to 365-day retention.

A serverless orchestration service using state machines to coordinate Lambda functions and AWS services — supporting sequential, parallel, branching, error handling, retry, and wait states with visual workflow monitoring.

Standard workflows run up to 1 year with exactly-once execution and full history (for long-running). Express workflows run up to 5 minutes with at-least-once execution at high volume (for event processing, streaming).

A serverless event bus for building event-driven architectures — routing events from AWS services, SaaS apps, and custom sources to targets based on rules and patterns, with schema registry and archive/replay capabilities.

A managed in-memory caching service supporting Redis and Memcached — reducing database load and improving response times for read-heavy workloads, session stores, leaderboards, and real-time analytics.

Redis: data persistence, replication, pub/sub, Lua scripting, sorted sets, Multi-AZ with auto-failover. Memcached: multi-threaded, simple key-value, no persistence, horizontal scaling via sharding — choose Redis for most use cases.

Lazy Loading (cache-aside): load data into cache only on cache miss — stale data possible but resilient. Write-Through: write to cache and DB simultaneously — never stale but write penalty. TTL: set expiration to balance freshness and performance.

The service controlling who (authentication) can do what (authorization) in AWS — using users, groups, roles, and JSON policies with Effect/Action/Resource/Condition to enforce least-privilege access.

JSON documents defining permissions — Identity-based (attached to users/groups/roles), Resource-based (attached to resources like S3 buckets), and Permission boundaries (maximum permissions limit). Evaluated as: explicit Deny > explicit Allow > implicit Deny.

Temporary credential sets assumed by AWS services, applications, or users — no long-term credentials, automatically rotated by STS. Used for EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, cross-account access, and federation.

A service that provides temporary, limited-privilege credentials — AssumeRole (cross-account), AssumeRoleWithSAML (enterprise SSO), AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity (social/OIDC login), GetSessionToken (MFA-protected API access).

A managed user directory for application sign-up/sign-in — providing username/password authentication, MFA, social identity federation (Google, Facebook, Apple), SAML/OIDC enterprise federation, and JWT token issuance.

A service that provides temporary AWS credentials to authenticated and guest users — mapping Cognito User Pool tokens, social logins, or SAML assertions to IAM roles for direct access to AWS services (S3, DynamoDB).

Term: Cognito User Pools vs Identity Pools
Definition: User Pools handle authentication (who are you?) and return JWTs for API access. Identity Pools handle authorization (what can you access?) and return temporary AWS credentials. Often used together: User Pool authenticates → Identity Pool authorizes.

IAM authorization (SigV4 signing), Lambda authorizers (custom token/request validation returning IAM policies), Cognito User Pool authorizers (JWT validation), and API keys (for usage plans, not security) — choose based on client type and requirements.

A Lambda function that API Gateway invokes to validate bearer tokens or request parameters — returning an IAM policy that determines access. Supports token-based (JWT/OAuth) and request-based (headers, query strings) authorization with result caching.

A managed service for creating and controlling encryption keys — symmetric (AES-256) and asymmetric (RSA, ECC) keys with key policies, automatic rotation, and CloudTrail audit logging of all key usage.

A two-tier encryption pattern — KMS generates a data key, the data key encrypts your data (client-side), and KMS encrypts the data key with a CMK. Only the encrypted data key is stored alongside the ciphertext, enabling encryption of data larger than 4 KB.

Resource-based policies attached to KMS keys that define who can use and manage the key — the primary access control mechanism for KMS keys, required even when IAM policies grant access (both must allow).

A service that stores, rotates, and retrieves secrets (database credentials, API keys, tokens) — with automatic rotation via Lambda, cross-account access, and SDK integration for runtime secret retrieval without hardcoding.

A hierarchical key-value store for configuration data and secrets — supporting String, StringList, and SecureString (KMS-encrypted) types. Free tier available, integrated with IAM, CloudFormation, and Lambda for configuration management.

Secrets Manager: built-in rotation, higher cost, designed for secrets. Parameter Store: free tier, hierarchical organization, no built-in rotation (use Lambda), supports both config and secrets — choose Secrets Manager when automatic rotation is required.

Server-side: SSE-S3 (Amazon-managed keys, default), SSE-KMS (KMS keys with audit trail and separate permissions), SSE-C (customer-provided keys). Client-side: encrypt before upload. Enforce with bucket policies requiring encryption headers.

Resource-based JSON policies controlling bucket and object access — supporting conditions like IP restrictions, VPC endpoint restrictions, MFA requirements, encryption enforcement, and cross-account access grants.

A browser security mechanism that controls which domains can access resources from another domain — configured on S3 buckets and API Gateway to allow web applications hosted on one domain to call APIs or access assets on another.

A service that provisions and manages SSL/TLS certificates — free public certificates that auto-renew, integrated with ALB, CloudFront, and API Gateway for HTTPS encryption without manual certificate management.

A Layer 7 firewall protecting web applications — attached to CloudFront, ALB, or API Gateway with rules for SQL injection, XSS, rate limiting, IP blocking, geo-blocking, and AWS Managed Rules for common threats.

A PaaS that deploys and manages web applications — handling capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and health monitoring while you upload code. Supports Docker, Java, .NET, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, and PHP.

All at once (fastest, downtime), Rolling (batches, reduced capacity), Rolling with additional batch (maintains capacity), Immutable (new ASG, safest), Traffic splitting (canary testing) — choose based on speed vs. safety tradeoffs.

YAML/JSON configuration files in a .ebextensions/ folder that customize Beanstalk environments — installing packages, creating files, configuring services, setting environment properties, and defining custom resources.

An IaC service that provisions AWS resources using declarative JSON/YAML templates — defining resources, parameters, mappings, conditions, outputs, and dependencies for repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure deployment.

Built-in functions for dynamic template values — Ref (reference resources/parameters), Fn::GetAtt (get resource attributes), Fn::Join (concatenate strings), Fn::Sub (substitute variables), Fn::Select, Fn::If, and Fn::ImportValue.

Automatic rollback to the previous state when stack creation or update fails — all resources are deleted (creation) or reverted (update) to maintain consistency. Can be disabled for debugging failed deployments.

A preview of proposed changes before executing a stack update — showing which resources will be added, modified, or replaced, enabling review and approval before applying potentially disruptive infrastructure changes.
Stacks created as resources within other stacks — enabling reuse of common template patterns (VPC, ALB, security groups) across multiple parent stacks, with lifecycle managed by the parent stack.

An extension of CloudFormation that simplifies serverless application deployment — providing shorthand syntax for Lambda functions, API Gateway APIs, DynamoDB tables, and Step Functions with local testing via SAM CLI.

A command-line tool for building, testing, and deploying SAM applications locally — sam init (scaffold), sam build (compile), sam local invoke (test Lambda), sam local start-api (test API Gateway), and sam deploy (deploy to AWS).

An IaC framework that defines cloud infrastructure using programming languages (TypeScript, Python, Java, C#, Go) — synthesizing to CloudFormation templates, with constructs (L1, L2, L3) for reusable infrastructure patterns.

A fully managed Git-based source control service — hosting private repositories with encryption at rest and in transit, IAM-based access control, and integration with CodePipeline for CI/CD workflows.

A fully managed continuous integration service that compiles code, runs tests, and produces deployable artifacts — using buildspec.yml to define build phases (install, pre_build, build, post_build) in Docker containers.

A YAML file defining build commands and settings — specifying runtime versions, environment variables (plaintext and Secrets Manager/Parameter Store references), build phases, artifacts output, and cache configuration.

A managed deployment service for EC2, on-premises, Lambda, and ECS — supporting in-place (rolling) and blue/green deployments with automatic rollback, deployment groups, lifecycle hooks, and appspec.yml configuration.

A YAML file defining deployment instructions — for EC2: file mappings and lifecycle hook scripts (BeforeInstall, AfterInstall, ApplicationStart, ValidateService). For Lambda: function version and traffic shifting configuration.

A fully managed CI/CD orchestration service — defining stages (Source, Build, Test, Deploy) with actions that connect CodeCommit, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CloudFormation, and third-party tools into automated release pipelines.

A deployment strategy that runs two identical environments — Blue (current) and Green (new). Traffic is switched from Blue to Green after validation, enabling instant rollback by switching back. Supported by CodeDeploy, Elastic Beanstalk, and Route 53.

A deployment strategy that routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version first — monitoring for errors before gradually shifting all traffic. Lambda aliases support 10%/90% weighted routing; CodeDeploy supports canary configurations.

A fully managed Docker container registry for storing, managing, and deploying container images — with image scanning for vulnerabilities, lifecycle policies for cleanup, cross-region/cross-account replication, and IAM-based access control.
A fully managed container orchestration service — running Docker containers on EC2 instances or Fargate (serverless), with task definitions, services, load balancer integration, and auto-scaling.

JSON blueprints for running containers in ECS — specifying Docker image, CPU/memory, port mappings, environment variables, IAM task role, log configuration, and volumes. Each revision is immutable and versioned.

Term: ECS - Fargate vs EC2 Launch Type
Definition: Fargate: serverless, no EC2 management, pay per task, automatic scaling. EC2: you manage instances, more control over infrastructure, can use Spot/Reserved instances for cost savings — choose Fargate for simplicity, EC2 for control.

A service that collects, stores, and analyzes log data from Lambda, EC2, ECS, API Gateway, and other sources — with log groups, log streams, metric filters, subscription filters, and Logs Insights for querying.

Time-series data points published by AWS services and custom applications — standard metrics (5-minute intervals, free) and detailed monitoring (1-minute, paid). Custom metrics via PutMetricData API with dimensions for filtering.
Automated alerts based on metric thresholds — states: OK, ALARM, INSUFFICIENT_DATA. Actions: SNS notifications, Auto Scaling, EC2 actions. Composite alarms combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic.

A distributed tracing service that analyzes and debugs microservices — visualizing request flow through services (service map), identifying bottlenecks, and tracking errors across Lambda, API Gateway, ECS, EC2, and Elastic Beanstalk.

Segments represent a service processing a request (name, ID, timing, HTTP data). Subsegments provide granular detail within a segment (downstream calls, SQL queries, custom annotations). Together they form a complete trace.
Rules that control which requests are traced to reduce cost — default: 1 request/second + 5% of additional requests. Custom rules can target specific paths, methods, or services with different rates.

Annotations are indexed key-value pairs (string, number, boolean) used for filtering traces in the console. Metadata is non-indexed key-value data (any type) for storing additional information without search capability.

A background process that collects trace data from the X-Ray SDK and sends it to the X-Ray API — runs on EC2, ECS (sidecar container), or Elastic Beanstalk (auto-installed). Lambda has X-Ray built-in (enable Active Tracing).

A governance service that logs all API calls in your AWS account — recording who made the call, when, from where (IP), and what was requested. Essential for security auditing, compliance, and troubleshooting permission issues.

Common issues: timeout (max 15 min, check downstream services), out of memory (increase memory setting), permission denied (check execution role), throttling (check concurrency limits), and cold starts (use Provisioned Concurrency).

4xx client errors: 400 (bad request), 403 (forbidden/WAF), 429 (throttled). 5xx server errors: 502 (bad gateway/Lambda error), 503 (service unavailable), 504 (integration timeout, 29-second limit). Check CloudWatch logs for details.

ProvisionedThroughputExceededException when RCU/WCU limits are exceeded — caused by hot partitions, large items, or burst traffic. Solutions: exponential backoff, DAX caching, on-demand capacity, better partition key design.

Multi-part upload for large files, S3 Transfer Acceleration for distant uploads, byte-range fetches for parallel downloads, CloudFront for read caching, and prefix-based parallelism (3,500 PUT/5,500 GET per prefix per second).

Messages not being deleted (check VisibilityTimeout vs processing time), duplicate processing (use FIFO or idempotency), messages going to DLQ (check maxReceiveCount and consumer errors), and empty receives (enable long polling).

A retry strategy where wait time doubles with each retry attempt plus random jitter — preventing thundering herd problems when many clients retry simultaneously. Built into AWS SDKs for throttled API calls.

A service that inspects your AWS environment and provides recommendations across five categories — cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits — helping optimize your AWS infrastructure.

An open-source tool that runs your Lambda function at different memory configurations to find the optimal balance of cost and performance — since Lambda CPU scales proportionally with memory allocation.
A feature that analyzes log data to identify top contributors — finding the most active IP addresses, URLs, or DynamoDB partition keys causing throttling, enabling targeted optimization of hot spots.
